26: venathati lachem lev hadash veruach hadasha etten bekirbechem vahashirothi et-lev haeben mivsarchem venatati lachem lev basar: 'And I will give you a new heart and a new spirit I will put within you. And I will remove the heart of stone from your flesh and give you a heart of flesh.' The new heart-new spirit promise is the Ezekielian new covenant (parallel to Jer 31:31-34). Lev hadash (new heart): the decision-making center (lev) of human personhood is replaced — not repaired, not improved, but new. Ruach hadasha (new spirit): YHWH's own Spirit placed within (v. 27: 'I will put my Spirit within you and cause you to walk in my statutes'). This is Pentecost prophesied — the Spirit's indwelling that replaces external Torah-motivation with internal Spirit-empowered desire and ability to obey.
1: veatah ben-adam hinabbe el-harei yisrael ('son of man, prophesy to the mountains of Israel'): the mountains of Israel are addressed directly as the recipients of the restoration oracle. Ch 36 opens as the counterpart to the ch 6 judgment oracle that was also addressed to the mountains (6:2-3). The mountains that received the sword, famine, and plague of judgment now receive the promise of life and flourishing.
2: yaan amar ha'oyev aleichem heach bamot olam ('the enemy has said of you: Aha! the ancient heights have become our possession'): Edom (from ch 35) and other enemies have exulted over Israel's desolated heights. bamot olam ('ancient/eternal high places') — the mountains Israel has inhabited since the patriarchal era. The enemy's gloating ('heach!' = a cry of malicious satisfaction) is the theological occasion for YHWH's response.
3: yaan yaan shomate ('because — yes, because you are desolate'): the doubled yaan for emphasis — a construction that intensifies the causal connection. The desolation of the land (from the Babylonian conquest) is real and acknowledged; YHWH's oracle does not minimize it. But the enemy's contemptuous seizure and mockery of Israel's land becomes an affront to YHWH that generates the restoration.
4: The comprehensive address: mountains, hills, ravines, valleys, desolate wastes, abandoned cities — the whole topography of ruined Judah is addressed. The oracle covers every part of the landscape, leaving no part of the land excluded from the restoration promise. The specificity of the address (seven categories of terrain) emphasizes the thoroughness of the coming transformation.
5: beeesh qin'ati dibbarti ('in the fire of my jealousy I have spoken'): qin'ah (H7068, jealousy/zeal) — YHWH's jealousy is not a weakness but an expression of covenant fidelity. The same qin'ah that executed judgment on Israel for idolatry (5:13; 8:3) now burns against the nations that have seized the covenant land. yaan shim'chat kal-levav uveuzat naphesh legarsh otah — 'because of wholehearted joy and bitter contempt with which they made my land their possession': the malice and contempt (not merely opportunism) of the nations' action triggers the jealous response.
6: davari al-admat yisrael ('speak about the land of Israel'): YHWH's jealousy for his land — the land is inseparably connected to his covenant identity. The nations' contempt for Israel is simultaneously contempt for YHWH, since the land is his. This theological point drives the restoration: YHWH acts to vindicate his own reputation, not merely to restore Israel out of sentiment.
7: im-lo haggoyim asher-lachem misaviv hemah yesu kalimatam ('the nations around you will themselves bear their disgrace'): the reversal-formula — those who shamed Israel will be shamed. The same vocabulary (kalimah, H3639, shame/disgrace) applied to Israel in exile is redirected to the surrounding nations. YHWH's oath (nesa yadi, 'I have raised my hand') formalizes the reversal.
8: veaitem titenu anpechem uvliyitem piriechem ('you mountains will put forth your branches and bear your fruit for my people Israel'): the mountains addressed as living entities bearing fruit — the same personification as in ch 6 (where they were addressed as recipients of judgment). The mountains are commanded to participate in the restoration: ki qerovim lavo ('for they are soon to come'). The returning exiles are the 'soon' — the restoration is not distant.
9: ki hineni eleichem upeniti aleichem venirashtem venigraztem ('I will turn to you and you will be tilled and sown'): panah el (H6437, to turn toward/face toward) — YHWH's face turning toward the mountains as the active agent of restoration. Tilling and sowing are the signs of cultivated settlement versus the desolation of abandoned fields. The agricultural renewal is the sign of human return.
10: vehirbeiti aleichem adam ('I will multiply people on you'): the reversal of the depopulation caused by exile, sword, and famine. venishvu hecheharim uhivnyu habamot ('the ruined cities will be inhabited and the waste places built'): charubot (H2723, ruins/waste places) repopulated — the archaeological landscape of conquest reversed by divine restoration.
11: vehirbeiti aleichem adam uvehemah verebu veparu ('I will multiply people and animals on you; they will increase and be fruitful'): the Genesis 1:28 fertility mandate (peru urevu, 'be fruitful and multiply') applied to the restored land. The restoration recapitulates creation: the fruitfulness that was interrupted by the exile-curse is restarted by YHWH's direct action. kaqdemonichem asiti ('I will make you as in your former state'): restoration to pre-exile conditions, but — as subsequent verses clarify — actually surpassing the former state.
12: veholachti aleichem adam et-ami yisrael ('I will bring my people Israel on you'): the possessive construction — ami yisrael ('my people Israel') emphasizes the covenant relationship that the exile appeared to have dissolved. veyerishuch ('they will possess you'): the land-possession verb (yaresh, H3423) that constitutes the fulfillment of the ancestral promises from Num 13-14 through Deut 30:5.
13: koh amar adonai YHWH yaan yomerim lach ochelet adam at ('because they say of you: you devour human beings'): the accusation against the land — that it consumes its inhabitants (a pagan understanding of why successive peoples were expelled from Canaan). ocheleth adam — the land as a man-eater, like the slanderous report of the spies (Num 13:32: 'the land devours its inhabitants'). YHWH addresses and denies this accusation about his own land.
14: lachen adam lo-tocheeli od uggoyech lo-teshakeli od ('you will no longer devour human beings and no longer bereave your nation'): YHWH's direct rebuttal of the accusation — the land will not 'eat' its inhabitants. The implied theology: the expulsions and deaths were not the land's inherent hostility but YHWH's specific judgments that have now run their course. The land's character is restored alongside the people's restoration.
15: velo-ashmi'a elayich od klimat haggoyim ('I will no longer cause you to hear the insults of the nations'): the end of the international shame that accompanied exile. cherpath ammim lo-tis'i od ('you will no longer bear the reproach of the peoples'): cherpah (H2781, reproach/shame) — the same shame that Job mourned and the Psalms cry out against. The restoration removes not just the physical desolation but the shame that accompanied it.
16: The shift to the theological explanation of exile (vv.16-21) — the 'because' section before the 'therefore' restoration. YHWH speaks directly: the exile happened because of Israel's conduct and deeds.
17: ben-adam beit yisrael yoshvim al-admatam vayetamu'u otah bedarkam uvealilotam ('son of man, when the house of Israel dwelt in their own land, they defiled it by their ways and their deeds'): the historical summary of why the land was given over. ketumah haniddah hayetah darkam lefanai ('their way was before me like the impurity of a menstruous woman'): niddah (H5079, menstrual impurity/isolation period) — the strongest cultic impurity language in the Levitical system (Lev 15:19-30). Israel's conduct was ritually defiling to the land itself. The land as covenant space shared YHWH's holiness and could not tolerate Israel's conduct.
18: vaeshpoch chamati aleichem ('I poured out my wrath on them'): shaphach chemah — the standard Ezekielian phrase for divine wrath-execution. al-hadam asher-shapechu al-haaretz ('for the blood they shed on the land'): blood-pollution of the land (Num 35:33: blood pollutes the land) — the legal basis for the exile. The land could not tolerate innocent blood; YHWH executed the legal requirement of expulsion.
19: vaeputzem baggoyim vayyizaru baarasot ('I scattered them among the nations and they were dispersed through the countries'): the Deuteronomic curse executed (Deut 4:27; 28:64). The scatter-verb putz (H6327) and zarah (H2219, winnow/scatter) — both agricultural metaphors for the violent dispersal of a community.
20: vayavo el-haggoyim asher-bau sham vayehalelu et-shem qodshi ('when they came to the nations where they went, they profaned my holy name'): the remarkable claim — Israel's continued existence as a scattered people was itself a profanation of YHWH's name. chilel shem qodshi (H2490 + H8034 + H6944, profane holy name) — the nations observed exiled Israel and concluded 'YHWH could not protect his people.' The exile that was meant to honor YHWH's justice was being interpreted by the nations as YHWH's weakness or failure.
21: vaechamal al-shem qodshi ('I had concern/compassion for my holy name'): chamal (H2550, spare/have compassion/be concerned for) — YHWH's motivation for the restoration is explicitly his concern for his own name's reputation, not Israel's merit. The restoration is an act of theological self-vindication. This is the most theologically direct statement of YHWH's motivation in the entire OT: the nations were getting the wrong message; YHWH acts to correct it.
22: lachen emor leveit yisrael koh amar adonai YHWH lo lemankhem ani oseh beit yisrael ki im-leshmi haqadosh ('therefore say to Israel: not for your sake am I acting, house of Israel, but for my holy name'): the explicit statement of YHWH's motivation — emphatically not for Israel's sake but for his own name. lo lemankhem is placed first for maximum emphasis. This is the theological anti-sentimentalism of the OT: the restoration is not sentimental return because YHWH loves Israel; it is YHWH acting to vindicate his own reputation among the nations. Israel is the vehicle, not the ultimate purpose.
23: veqiddashti et-shemi hagadol hamechullal baggoyim ('I will sanctify my great name which has been profaned among the nations'): qiddesh (H6942, sanctify/hallow) versus chilel (H2490, profane) — the key antithesis of this oracle. veyadu haggoyim ki-ani YHWH: the recognition formula applied to the nations — the restoration of Israel serves YHWH's self-disclosure to the entire world. behiqadshi bachem leeyneichem ('when I show myself holy in you before their eyes'): YHWH's holiness displayed in Israel's restoration functions as a sermon to the nations about who YHWH is.
24: velaqachti etchem min-haggoyim ('I will take you from the nations'): the second Exodus vocabulary — laqach (take/gather) from the nations parallels the first Exodus from Egypt. uqibatzti etchem mikol-haaratzot ('and gather you from all the lands'): the ingathering of the scattered, reversing the diaspora of v.19. The return from Babylon is the near fulfillment; the ultimate fulfillment awaits the complete ingathering.
25: vezaraqti aleichem mayim tehorim uteharthem ('I will sprinkle clean water on you and you will be clean'): zaraq (H2236, sprinkle — the priestly sprinkling verb used in sacrificial purification, Lev 1:5; Num 19:13-20; the red heifer waters). The purification of the restored people is accomplished by YHWH himself performing the priestly act of sprinkling. mikol-tum'oteichem umikol-gilluleichem ataher etchem ('from all your impurities and from all your idols I will cleanse you'): comprehensive cleansing covering both ritual impurity (tum'ah) and moral/idolatrous pollution (gillulim).
27: vet-ruchi eten beqirbechem ('I will put my Spirit within you'): the interior dimension of the new covenant — the Spirit placed beqerev (within, in the midst of). ruach (H7307, spirit/breath/wind). The new heart of v.26 receives the animating Spirit of v.27. veeth-chuqqotai tishmeiru vaasitem ('I will cause you to walk in my statutes and you will keep my ordinances and do them'): the Spirit's work is specifically defined as enabling Torah-observance. The failure of the old covenant (Israel could not keep the commandments) is addressed by the interior gift of the Spirit who works obedience from within.
28: veyashavtem baaretz asher natati laavoteichem ('you will dwell in the land I gave to your fathers'): the land-covenant renewed. vihyitem li le'am vaani ehyeh lachem le'Elohim ('you will be my people and I will be your God'): the covenant formula (H1961 + H5971 + H430) — the most fundamental statement of covenant relationship in the OT (from Lev 26:12 through Jer 31:33; Ezek 11:20; 14:11; Rev 21:3). The covenant threatened and dissolved by the exile is reconstituted under the new heart/Spirit arrangement.
29: vehoshati mechal-tum'oteichem ('I will save you from all your uncleanness'): yasha (H3467, save) — the salvation vocabulary applied to the cleansing from impurity. Salvation and purification are linked: the same act that restores the covenant relationship removes the defilement that interrupted it. veqarati el-hadagan vehirbiti oto ('I will call to the grain and multiply it'): YHWH summoning agricultural abundance — the reversal of the covenant curses of famine and blight.
30: The restoration of agricultural abundance — fruit, grain, the end of famine. The reversal of the curses of Lev 26 and Deut 28: the land will produce its fruit so that Israel will no longer suffer the disgrace of famine among the nations. The nations that noted Israel's famine as evidence of divine abandonment will see its reversal as evidence of divine restoration.
31: uzchartem et-darkeichem hara'im ('you will remember your evil ways'): the remembrance of sin as part of the restoration, not as pre-condition but as consequence. Israel will remember its evil ways and deeds and loathe itself (qot, H6962, feel loathing/disgust) in its own sight. Genuine restoration produces self-abasement, not self-satisfaction. lo-lemaanchem again — 'not for your sake': the restoration does not generate pride in Israel but self-examination and shame.
32: lo lemankhem ani oseh ('it is not for your sake that I am acting'): the formula repeated from v.22 — the divine motivation is reasserted at the conclusion of the restoration oracle. The repetition brackets the entire oracle of vv.22-32 with the same theological claim. YHWH acts for his own name; Israel is the beneficiary but not the cause. boshtem vekhalmuni ('be ashamed and humiliated'): shame as the appropriate response to undeserved grace — the restoration should produce not pride but deep shame at the contrast between what Israel deserves and what YHWH gives.
33: koh amar adonai YHWH beyom tahhari etchem miqqol avonoteichem ('on the day I cleanse you from all your iniquities'): the day of cleansing as the trigger for the restoration of the cities. YHWH's act of moral cleansing and the act of urban reconstruction are simultaneous — the inner renewal produces the outer restoration. The land's desolation was a consequence of Israel's moral defilement; cleansing produces rebuilding.
34: vehaadamah hanneshamah teaved tachath asher haytah shemamah le-eynei kol avar ('the desolate land will be tilled instead of being a desolation in the sight of all who pass by'): the public nature of the restoration — those who passed by and saw the desolation as evidence of divine abandonment will see the tilled land as evidence of divine restoration. The restoration is a visible theological statement to the watching world.
35: vaomru haaretz halezut hayeta keGan-Eden ('and they will say: this land that was desolate has become like the garden of Eden'): Gan-Eden (the garden of Eden) as the highest possible standard of agricultural beauty and fertility. The restored land compared to Eden is the ultimate statement of what YHWH intends for the covenant land — not merely pre-exile normalcy but Edenic abundance. The garden destroyed by the Serpent and closed by cherubim is the type for the restoration.
36: veyadu haggoyim ki ani YHWH ('the nations will know that I am the LORD'): the recognition formula on the lips of the nations — their observation of Eden-like Israel will produce knowledge of YHWH. The nations learn who YHWH is not through theological argument but through observable transformation of Israel's land. baniti hacharubot natati nezerot ('I YHWH have rebuilt the ruined places and replanted the desolate areas'): YHWH as builder and farmer — the divine craftsman who rebuilds what was demolished and the divine gardener who replants what was uprooted.
37: The parabolic comparison: Israel's small population (like a few sheep in Jerusalem's pasture, before the exile) versus the restored great flocks filling the cities. The restored cities compared to the festive sheep-market in Jerusalem before major feasts — dense, noisy, full of life. The desolation reversed into abundance that exceeds the former state.
38: veyadu ki-ani YHWH: the chapter closes with the recognition formula for the seventh and final time in ch 36. The seven-fold repetition of yadu ki-ani YHWH in this chapter is structural: the entire oracle is oriented toward the epistemological outcome. Every act of restoration — cleansing, Spirit-giving, agricultural abundance, land rebuilding — serves this single end: that YHWH will be known as YHWH.
